schools.summary: God's purpose in hardening Pharaoh's heart: 'and the LORD said unto Moses, Go in unto Pharaoh: for I have hardened his heart, and the heart of his servants, that I might shew these my signs before him' — God declaring he had hardened Pharaoh's heart (and his servants'), in the judicial hardening of the obstinate king (the hardening foretold, Exodus 4:21; 7:3, following Pharaoh's own self-hardening, Exodus 8:15, 32, the divine sovereignty over the heart of the proud king); the purpose — 'that I might shew these my signs before him': God hardening Pharaoh that he might display his signs and wonders (the plagues, the deliverance, the purpose of the hardening, the multiplying of the signs, Exodus 7:3-4, the showing of God's power, Exodus 9:16, the glory of God in the judgments, the providence overruling the king's obstinacy for God's own ends, the manifestation of his power and the instruction of his people, the sovereign purpose, Romans 9:17-18, 'that I might shew my power in thee... whom he will he hardeneth'); God's hardening (the judicial giving-up of the obstinate to their own obstinacy, after long resistance to mercy and warning) — the purpose (the signs displayed, the power shown, the people instructed, vv.2, the providence overruling the hardened heart) — Calvin, Henry, Wesley.

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, Go in unto Pharaoh: for I have hardened his heart, and the heart of his servants, that I might shew these my signs before him
God's purpose in hardening Pharaoh's heart
the judicial hardening of the obstinate king (Exodus 4:21; 7:3; after his self-hardening, 8:15, 32)
overruled for the displaying of God's power (Exodus 9:16; Romans 9:17-18)

schools.summary: The signs to be told to generations: 'and that thou mayest tell in the ears of thy son, and of thy son's son, what things I have wrought in Egypt, and my signs which I have done among them; that ye may know how that I am the LORD' — the great word — 'that thou mayest tell in the ears of thy son, and of thy son's son, what things I have wrought in Egypt': a further purpose of the plagues — that the mighty works of God in Egypt might be told to the children and the children's children, from generation to generation (the catechetical purpose, the parents rehearsing God's wonders to their children, the foundation of the Passover instruction, Exodus 12:26-27; 13:8, 14; Deuteronomy 6:20-21; Psalm 78:4-7, 'we will not hide them from their children, shewing to the generation to come the praises of the LORD,' the handing-down of the knowledge of God's redemptive acts, the works of God recounted that faith might be passed on); the 'things wrought' and 'signs' (the plagues, the deliverance) — the great redemptive acts to be remembered and told (the matter of the children's instruction, the wonders of God recounted); 'that ye may know how that I am the LORD' (the end of the telling: that Israel, and their generations, might know the LORD, his power, faithfulness, and covenant, the knowledge of God passed on through the rehearsing of his works, the purpose of the signs, the knowledge of the LORD, Exodus 6:7; 7:5) — Calvin, Clarke, Gill, Ellicott.

debates.question: What is the purpose of the signs in v.2?

sides.position: The catechetical purpose - that the redemptive acts be told to the children's children from generation to generation (Exodus 12:26-27; 13:8; Deuteronomy 6:20-21; Psalm 78:4-7), the knowledge of God ('that ye may know I am the LORD') handed down through the telling of his works

themes: And that thou mayest tell in the ears of thy son, and of thy son's son, what things I have wrought in Egypt... that ye may know how that I am the LORD
the signs to be told to generations (the crux of the catechesis)
the catechetical purpose - the redemptive acts handed down to the children (Exodus 12:26-27; 13:8; Psalm 78:4-7)
the knowledge of God passed on through the telling of his works

schools.summary: God's reproof of Pharaoh's pride: 'and Moses and Aaron came in unto Pharaoh, and said unto him, Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews, How long wilt thou refuse to humble thyself before me? let my people go, that they may serve me' — God reproving Pharaoh for his persistent pride and refusal to humble himself before the LORD (the rebuke of the obstinate king, who would not bow despite the plagues, the call to humility, the very thing the proud king refused, the divine remonstrance); the refusal to humble himself (the root sin of Pharaoh, the pride that would not submit to God) — the obstinacy (the call to repentance refused, the proud heart unbroken by the judgments); 'let my people go, that they may serve me' (the renewed demand, Exodus 5:1; 8:1, 20, the release pressed, before the plague of locusts, the worship of God the end, the demand joined to the reproof) — Calvin, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And Moses and Aaron came in unto Pharaoh, and said unto him, Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews, How long wilt thou refuse to humble thyself before me? let my people go, that they may serve me
God's reproof of Pharaoh's pride
the rebuke of the obstinate king who would not humble himself
the call to the humility he refused, the demand pressed (Exodus 8:1)

schools.summary: The threat of the plague of locusts: 'else, if thou refuse to let my people go, behold, to morrow will I bring the locusts into thy coast' — God threatening, if Pharaoh refused, to bring the locusts upon Egypt on the morrow (the eighth plague announced, the locusts devouring all the land, vv.5-6, the warning before the judgment, the plague conditioned on the refusal); the locusts (the devouring swarms, a dreaded plague in the East, Joel 1:4; 2:25) — the eighth plague (the judgment that would consume all that the hail had left, v.5, the dreaded scourge); 'into thy coast (border, territory)' (the locusts upon all Egypt, the all-devouring plague, the judgment, the land stripped bare, v.15, the providence, the plague following the refusal) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott.

themes: Else, if thou refuse to let my people go, behold, to morrow will I bring the locusts into thy coast
the threat of the plague of locusts
the eighth plague announced, the all-devouring scourge (Joel 1:4)
conditioned on the refusal, the land to be stripped bare (v.15)

schools.summary: The locusts devouring all that the hail had left: 'and they shall cover the face of the earth, that one cannot be able to see the earth: and they shall eat the residue of that which is escaped, which remaineth unto you from the hail, and shall eat every tree which groweth for you out of the field' — the locusts covering the whole face of the earth (so thick none could see the ground) and devouring all that the hail (the seventh plague, Exodus 9:25) had left (the all-devouring plague, the completing of the destruction begun by the hail, the judgment stripping the land bare); the locusts covering the ground (so thick the earth could not be seen) — the vast swarms (the all-covering plague); 'eat the residue... and shall eat every tree which groweth for you out of the field' (the locusts devouring all the remaining crops and trees, what the hail had spared, the complete stripping of the land, the judgment leaving nothing green, v.15, the providence, the plague that would force Pharaoh's relenting, v.16) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And they shall cover the face of the earth, that one cannot be able to see the earth: and they shall eat the residue of that which is escaped, which remaineth unto you from the hail, and shall eat every tree
the locusts devouring all that the hail had left
the all-devouring plague completing the destruction begun by the hail (Exodus 9:25)
the land stripped bare, leaving nothing green (v.15)

schools.summary: The unprecedented plague and Moses' departure: 'and they shall fill thy houses, and the houses of all thy servants, and the houses of all the Egyptians; which neither thy fathers, nor thy fathers' fathers have seen, since the day that they were upon the earth unto this day. And he turned himself, and went out from Pharaoh' — the locusts would fill even the houses (not only the fields), a plague unprecedented (greater than any the Egyptians' fathers had seen, vv.14), and Moses, having delivered the warning, turning and going out from Pharaoh (the magnitude of the judgment, the departure of the prophet); the plague unlike any before (greater than all former locust-plagues, v.14) — the extraordinary judgment (the unprecedented scourge); 'he turned himself, and went out from Pharaoh' (Moses leaving abruptly, the warning given, the message complete, the prophet's departure, perhaps in indignation at the king's obstinacy, the providence, the warning delivered, the plague to follow, vv.13-15) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And they shall fill thy houses... which neither thy fathers, nor thy fathers' fathers have seen... And he turned himself, and went out from Pharaoh
the unprecedented plague and Moses' departure
a plague greater than any the fathers had seen (v.14)
the prophet departing, the warning delivered (vv.13-15)

schools.summary: Pharaoh's servants pleading for Israel's release: 'and Pharaoh's servants said unto him, How long shall this man be a snare unto us? let the men go, that they may serve the LORD their God: knowest thou not yet that Egypt is destroyed?' — Pharaoh's own servants (the courtiers, lately hardened, Exodus 10:1) now pleading with him to let Israel go, calling Moses 'a snare' (a trap, the cause of Egypt's ruin, the appeal of the servants, the plagues having broken even their obstinacy, the pressure on the king from his own court); the servants' counsel (let Israel go) — the courtiers yielding before the king (the pressure of the ruined land, the appeal to reason); 'knowest thou not yet that Egypt is destroyed?' (the servants' remonstrance, Egypt already ruined by the plagues, the hail, Exodus 9:25, and worse to come, the obstinacy of the king exceeding even his hardened courtiers, they yielding, he not, the providence, the king's obstinacy isolating him even from his own) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And Pharaoh's servants said unto him, How long shall this man be a snare unto us? let the men go... knowest thou not yet that Egypt is destroyed?
Pharaoh's servants pleading for Israel's release
even the hardened courtiers yielding before the king
the king isolated in his obstinacy while Egypt lay ruined (Exodus 9:25)

schools.summary: Pharaoh recalling Moses to negotiate: 'and Moses and Aaron were brought again unto Pharaoh: and he said unto them, Go, serve the LORD your God: but who are they that shall go?' — Pharaoh, pressed by his servants (v.7), recalling Moses and Aaron and seeming to yield ('Go, serve the LORD'), but adding the question 'who are they that shall go?' — beginning to negotiate, to limit the concession (the seeming yielding, with a catch, the bargaining of the king); Pharaoh's apparent yielding ('Go, serve the LORD') — the seeming concession (the relenting under pressure, yet to be qualified, v.10-11); 'but who are they that shall go?' (Pharaoh's qualifying question, seeking to limit who should go, the bargaining, the prelude to the compromise, vv.10-11, the men only, the half-yielding, the concession with a condition, the providence, the king still resisting the full release) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Moses and Aaron were brought again unto Pharaoh: and he said unto them, Go, serve the LORD your God: but who are they that shall go?
Pharaoh recalling Moses to negotiate
the seeming concession qualified with a catch
the prelude to the compromise (vv.10-11)

schools.summary: Moses demanding the whole nation go: 'and Moses said, We will go with our young and with our old, with our sons and with our daughters, with our flocks and with our herds will we go; for we must hold a feast unto the LORD' — Moses demanding that the whole nation go: young and old, sons and daughters, flocks and herds — none left behind (the full demand, the complete release, no compromise, the whole people and their substance); even the flocks and herds must go (for the sacrifices, and as their property) — the complete release (no part left in Egypt, the full separation); 'for we must hold a feast unto the LORD' (the purpose, the feast, the worship of God, requiring all the people and their offerings, the demand grounded on God's claim, the full obedience, the whole nation set apart to serve God, no compromise) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin, Gill.

themes: And Moses said, We will go with our young and with our old, with our sons and with our daughters, with our flocks and with our herds will we go; for we must hold a feast unto the LORD
Moses demanding the whole nation go
the full release - young and old, flocks and herds, none left behind
the complete separation to worship God, no compromise

schools.summary: Pharaoh's mocking refusal of the full release: 'and he said unto them, Let the LORD be so with you, as I will let you go, and your little ones: look to it; for evil is before you' — Pharaoh mockingly refusing: he would as soon let them go with their little ones as the LORD would be with them (i.e., never, the sneering refusal, the proud king mocking both the demand and the LORD, the scornful rejection of the full release); Pharaoh's scornful words (mocking the LORD and the demand) — the contempt (the obstinacy, the king's pride sneering at God); 'look to it; for evil is before you' (Pharaoh's threat, some evil, mischief, or danger awaited them if they pressed the full demand, perhaps a charge that they meant rebellion or flight, the menace, the providence, the king's mockery and threat answered by the plague, vv.12-15) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And he said unto them, Let the LORD be so with you, as I will let you go, and your little ones: look to it; for evil is before you
Pharaoh's mocking refusal of the full release
the proud king sneering at both the demand and the LORD
the threat, answered by the plague (vv.12-15)

schools.summary: Pharaoh's third compromise - the men only: 'not so: go now ye that are men, and serve the LORD; for that ye did desire. And they were driven out from Pharaoh's presence' — Pharaoh offering a third partial concession — 'go now ye that are men, and serve the LORD; for that ye did desire': let the men go to worship, but not the little ones (the women and children, and the cattle, to be kept in Egypt, as hostages, that the men would return, the half-release, the men only, the families held back, the witnesses noting the lesson — another of the world's, and Satan's, compromises: let the grown serve God, but keep back the children, or, keep something back as a pledge of return to the world, serve God yourself, but leave your family in Egypt, the snare of the partial release, keeping the children, the future, in bondage, the families not wholly given to God, the partial obedience God's people must refuse, the whole household to be set apart, the children with the parents in the service of God, Exodus 10:9, the covenant including the children, Acts 2:39); Pharaoh's twisting (as if the men's worship were all they had asked) — the misrepresentation (the grudging concession); 'they were driven out from Pharaoh's presence' (Moses and Aaron driven out in anger, the king's wrath at the refusal of his compromise, the rupture, the providence, the king's obstinacy bringing the plague, vv.12-15) — Calvin, Gill.

debates.question: What is the snare in Pharaoh's 'go ye that are men' (keeping the children)?

sides.position: The third compromise (let the men serve God, keep the children in Egypt) - another of the world's snares: serve God yourself, but leave your family (children, future) in bondage; the partial release God's people must refuse, the whole household given to God (Exodus 10:9; the children of the covenant, Acts 2:39)

themes: Not so: go now ye that are men, and serve the LORD; for that ye did desire. And they were driven out from Pharaoh's presence
Pharaoh's third compromise - the men only (the crux of the half-release)
let the men serve God, but keep the children in Egypt as hostages
the world's snare - serve God but leave your family in bondage; the whole household to be given to God (Exodus 10:9; Acts 2:39)

schools.summary: The command for the plague of locusts: 'and the LORD said unto Moses, Stretch out thine hand over the land of Egypt for the locusts, that they may come up upon the land of Egypt, and eat every herb of the land, even all that the hail hath left' — God commanding Moses to stretch his hand over Egypt to bring the locusts, that they might devour every herb and all that the hail had left (the eighth plague executed, after the rupture, v.11, the judgment on the obstinate king, the all-devouring scourge); the locusts coming up (brought by God's hand, by the east wind, v.13) — the eighth plague (the devouring swarms); 'eat every herb of the land, even all that the hail hath left' (the locusts completing the destruction begun by the hail, Exodus 9:25, the land stripped bare, the judgment, nothing green left, v.15, the providence, the plague forcing Pharaoh's relenting, v.16) — Henry, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, Stretch out thine hand over the land of Egypt for the locusts... and eat every herb of the land, even all that the hail hath left
the command for the plague of locusts
the eighth plague executed after the rupture (v.11)
completing the destruction begun by the hail (Exodus 9:25)

schools.summary: The east wind bringing the locusts: 'and Moses stretched forth his rod over the land of Egypt, and the LORD brought an east wind upon the land all that day, and all that night; and when it was morning, the east wind brought the locusts' — Moses stretching out his rod, and the LORD bringing an east wind (blowing a day and a night) which brought the locusts in the morning (the means of the plague, the east wind, the natural agent in God's hand, bringing the swarms from the east, Arabia or beyond, the providence using natural means, the plague come); the east wind (the natural means God used to bring the locusts) — the providence (God ruling the winds to bring the plague, as he would the wind to remove them, v.19, the natural agent of the supernatural judgment); 'the east wind brought the locusts' (the locusts borne in by the wind, the vast swarms, the eighth plague come, the judgment, the land to be devoured, vv.14-15, the providence, the timing, the means, all of God) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And Moses stretched forth his rod over the land of Egypt, and the LORD brought an east wind... and the east wind brought the locusts
the east wind bringing the locusts
the providence using natural means (God ruling the winds, v.19)
the natural agent of the supernatural judgment, all of God

schools.summary: The unequalled swarm of locusts: 'and the locusts went up over all the land of Egypt, and rested in all the coasts of Egypt: very grievous were they; before them there were no such locusts as they, neither after them shall be such' — the locusts covering all Egypt, settling in every part, a swarm 'very grievous' (severe), unequalled before or after (the greatest locust-plague ever, v.6, the magnitude of the judgment, the extraordinary scourge); the 'very grievous' (heavy, severe) swarm — the dreadful plague (the all-covering locusts); 'before them there were no such locusts as they, neither after them shall be such' (the plague unequalled, the greatest in history, the like never before or after in Egypt, the extraordinary judgment, the providence, the unequalled plague forcing Pharaoh's relenting, v.16) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the locusts went up over all the land of Egypt, and rested in all the coasts of Egypt: very grievous were they; before them there were no such locusts as they, neither after them shall be such
the unequalled swarm of locusts
the greatest locust-plague ever, covering all Egypt (v.6)
the extraordinary judgment forcing Pharaoh's relenting (v.16)

schools.summary: The land stripped bare by the locusts: 'for they covered the face of the whole earth, so that the land was darkened; and they did eat every herb of the land, and all the fruit of the trees which the hail had left: and there remained not any green thing in the trees, or in the herbs of the field, through all the land of Egypt' — the locusts covering the ground (darkening the land by their multitude) and devouring every herb and all the fruit the hail had left, leaving not a green thing in all Egypt (the complete destruction, the land stripped utterly bare, the judgment fulfilled, the famine of the field); the land 'darkened' by the swarms (so thick they hid the ground, a foretaste of the darkness to come, v.21) — the vast multitude (the all-covering plague); 'there remained not any green thing in the trees, or in the herbs of the field' (the complete stripping of the land, nothing green left, total desolation, the judgment fulfilled, the providence, the ruin forcing Pharaoh's relenting, v.16) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: For they covered the face of the whole earth, so that the land was darkened... and there remained not any green thing in the trees, or in the herbs of the field, through all the land of Egypt
the land stripped bare by the locusts
the land darkened by the swarms, the utter stripping of Egypt
the complete desolation, the judgment fulfilled

schools.summary: Pharaoh's hasty confession of sin: 'then Pharaoh called for Moses and Aaron in haste; and he said, I have sinned against the LORD your God, and against you' — Pharaoh, alarmed by the ruin (the land stripped bare, v.15), hastily calling Moses and Aaron and confessing 'I have sinned against the LORD your God, and against you' (the confession under pressure, the proud king brought to own his sin, the haste, the urgency of the alarm); Pharaoh's words — 'I have sinned against the LORD your God, and against you': Pharaoh confessing sin (against God and against Moses and Aaron), yet the witnesses weighing it as a false or partial repentance — the confession of fear and necessity (wrung out by the plague), not of a broken, contrite heart, the confession that would vanish when the plague was removed (v.20, the lesson — the forced confession of the afflicted, as Saul, 1 Samuel 15:24, 30; Judas, Matthew 27:4, that is not true repentance, the words of confession without the change of heart, the repentance of the damned, owning sin under the lash, yet hardening again when relieved); 'and against you' (Pharaoh owning his wrong to Moses and Aaron, yet the confession of necessity, the providence, the confession that would not hold, the king relenting under pressure, hardening when relieved, v.20) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

debates.question: How is Pharaoh's 'I have sinned' to be regarded?

sides.position: A false or partial repentance - the confession of fear and necessity wrung out by the plague, not of a broken, contrite heart; the forced confession of the afflicted (as Saul, 1 Samuel 15:24; Judas, Matthew 27:4) that vanishes when the plague is removed (v.20)

themes: Then Pharaoh called for Moses and Aaron in haste; and he said, I have sinned against the LORD your God, and against you
Pharaoh's hasty confession of sin
the confession of fear and necessity, not of a contrite heart
the forced confession of the afflicted (as Saul, 1 Samuel 15:24), soon to vanish (v.20)

schools.summary: Pharaoh's plea for forgiveness and removal: 'now therefore forgive, I pray thee, my sin only this once, and intreat the LORD your God, that he may take away from me this death only' — Pharaoh begging forgiveness ('this once') and asking Moses to entreat the LORD to remove the plague (the locusts, 'this death,' the plea of necessity, the king begging relief, not truly repenting, the appeal to Moses' intercession); 'only this once' (the king's words betraying the falseness — seeking relief from this plague, not a changed heart) — the repentance of necessity (the plea that would not hold, the same false relenting as before, v.20); 'that he may take away from me this death only' (Pharaoh seeking only the removal of the plague, 'this death,' the locusts threatening famine and death, the relief, not the repentance, the providence, the plague removed, yet the king hardening again, v.20) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: Now therefore forgive, I pray thee, my sin only this once, and intreat the LORD your God, that he may take away from me this death only
Pharaoh's plea for forgiveness and removal
the plea of necessity, seeking relief not a changed heart
'this death only' - the locusts removed, yet the king hardening again (v.20)

schools.summary: Moses interceding for the removal of the locusts: 'and he went out from Pharaoh, and intreated the LORD' — Moses, despite the king's false repentance, going out and praying (entreating) the LORD to remove the locusts (the intercession, the deliverer praying for the very king who had driven him out, v.11, the readiness to forgive and pray, returning good for evil, the dependence on God); Moses leaving to pray — the intercessor's resort to God (the going out to entreat); 'intreated the LORD' (Moses praying for the removal, as before, Exodus 8:12, 30, the intercessor seeking the deliverance, the providence, God answering, the locusts removed, v.19, the prophet's mercy toward the obstinate king) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And he went out from Pharaoh, and intreated the LORD
Moses interceding for the removal of the locusts
praying for the very king who drove him out (v.11), returning good for evil
the intercessor's resort to God (Exodus 8:30)

schools.summary: The locusts swept away into the Red Sea: 'and the LORD turned a mighty strong west wind, which took away the locusts, and cast them into the Red sea; there remained not one locust in all the coasts of Egypt' — God turning a strong west wind which swept the locusts away and cast them into the Red Sea (the complete removal, 'not one locust' left, the providence, as the east wind brought them, v.13, the west wind removed them — all of God, the deliverance fulfilled); the west wind (the natural means God used to remove the locusts, as the east wind brought them) — the providence ruling the winds (the complete sweeping-away); 'cast them into the Red sea; there remained not one locust in all the coasts of Egypt' (the locusts drowned in the Red Sea, the same sea where Pharaoh's host would perish, Exodus 14:27-28, the complete removal, not one left, the deliverance, proving God's hand, the providence, the mercy that Pharaoh would abuse, v.20) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the LORD turned a mighty strong west wind, which took away the locusts, and cast them into the Red sea; there remained not one locust in all the coasts of Egypt
the locusts swept away into the Red Sea
the providence ruling the winds (as the east wind brought them, v.13)
the complete removal, the mercy Pharaoh would abuse (v.20)

schools.summary: Pharaoh hardened again after the locusts: 'but the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart, so that he would not let the children of Israel go' — Pharaoh, the locusts removed (v.19), hardened again (the LORD hardening, in the judicial giving-up of the obstinate, as Exodus 9:12; 10:1) and refusing to let Israel go (the broken confession, the false repentance vanishing when the plague was lifted, vv.16-17, the renewed obstinacy); the LORD hardening (the judicial hardening of the obstinate king, after his own self-hardening and his abuse of the mercies) — the divine giving-up (the obstinacy confirmed, Romans 1:24-28; 9:18); 'he would not let the children of Israel go' (the broken promise, the confession of v.16 vanished, the hardened refusal, the providence, the obstinacy bringing the next plague, the darkness, vv.21-23) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: But the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart, so that he would not let the children of Israel go
Pharaoh hardened again after the locusts
the judicial hardening of the obstinate king (Exodus 9:12; Romans 9:18)
the false confession vanishing when the plague was lifted (vv.16-17)

schools.summary: The command for the plague of darkness: 'and the LORD said unto Moses, Stretch out thine hand toward heaven, that there may be darkness over the land of Egypt, even darkness which may be felt' — God commanding Moses to stretch his hand toward heaven to bring darkness over Egypt (the ninth plague, the thick darkness, three days, vv.22-23, the judgment on Egypt, perhaps on the sun-god Ra, the chief deity, now blotted out, the supernatural darkness); the darkness (the supernatural, three-days' darkness, far beyond any natural gloom) — the judgment on Egypt's idolatry (the sun, moon, worshipped, now darkened, the terror of the darkness); 'even darkness which may be felt' (the darkness so thick and palpable it could, as it were, be felt, a dense, oppressive, terrifying gloom, perhaps with a thick, clammy mist, the supernatural horror, the judgment, a type of the outer darkness, Matthew 8:12, the darkness of God's wrath, the plagues of the end, Revelation 16:10) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, Stretch out thine hand toward heaven, that there may be darkness over the land of Egypt, even darkness which may be felt
the command for the plague of darkness
the ninth plague, the judgment on Egypt's idols (the sun-god darkened)
the thick, palpable gloom, a type of the outer darkness (Matthew 8:12; Revelation 16:10)

schools.summary: The three days' thick darkness: 'and Moses stretched forth his hand toward heaven; and there was a thick darkness in all the land of Egypt three days' — Moses stretching out his hand, and a thick darkness covering all Egypt for three days (the ninth plague executed, the supernatural, oppressive gloom, three days, the judgment fulfilled, the darkness over the land); the 'thick darkness' (the dense, palpable, supernatural gloom, v.21) — the terror of the plague (the all-covering darkness); 'three days' (the darkness lasting three full days, the prolonged, oppressive judgment, the terror, the Egyptians shut up in darkness, vv.23, the providence, the judgment on Egypt's idols, the contrast with Israel's light, v.23) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Moses stretched forth his hand toward heaven; and there was a thick darkness in all the land of Egypt three days
the three days' thick darkness
the ninth plague executed, the supernatural oppressive gloom
the prolonged judgment over all Egypt (v.23)

schools.summary: The darkness on Egypt while Israel had light: 'they saw not one another, neither rose any from his place for three days: but all the children of Israel had light in their dwellings' — the darkness so thick that the Egyptians could not see one another, and none stirred from his place (rooted, paralyzed, in the darkness) for three days (the terror and helplessness, the people frozen in the dark, unable to move or work, the oppressive, supernatural gloom); the great contrast — 'but all the children of Israel had light in their dwellings': while Egypt lay in thick darkness, Israel (in Goshen) having light in their dwellings (the marked distinction, as with the flies, Exodus 8:22; the murrain, Exodus 9:4-6, between God's people and the world, the witnesses noting the lesson — God distinguishes between his own and the world, Exodus 11:7; Malachi 3:18, giving light to his people in the day of the world's darkness, the type — light for God's people, darkness for the world, the children of light and the children of darkness, 1 Thessalonians 5:5, the light of God's countenance on his own while the world is in judgment, Isaiah 60:2, 'darkness shall cover the earth... but the LORD shall arise upon thee,' the providence sheltering his own in the day of judgment); 'all the children of Israel had light in their dwellings' (the special mercy, Israel exempt from the darkness, having light, the distinction of grace, the providence, the LORD distinguishing his people, a token of the covenant, the light of the redeemed) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

debates.question: What does Israel's light in the darkness display?

sides.position: The marked distinction between God's people and the world (Exodus 8:22; 11:7; Malachi 3:18) - light for the redeemed in the day of the world's darkness, the type of the children of light and the children of darkness (1 Thessalonians 5:5; Isaiah 60:2), the providence sheltering his own in the day of judgment

themes: They saw not one another, neither rose any from his place for three days: but all the children of Israel had light in their dwellings
the darkness on Egypt while Israel had light (the crux of the distinction)
the marked distinction between God's people and the world (Exodus 8:22; 11:7; Malachi 3:18)
light for the redeemed in the day of the world's darkness (1 Thessalonians 5:5; Isaiah 60:2)

schools.summary: Pharaoh's fourth compromise - leave the cattle: 'and Pharaoh called unto Moses, and said, Go ye, serve the LORD; only let your flocks and your herds be stayed: let your little ones also go with you' — Pharaoh, under the darkness, calling Moses and conceding more than before: now even the little ones might go (a step beyond the third compromise, v.11, the further yielding under the plague, the relenting growing under pressure); the snare — 'only let your flocks and your herds be stayed': Pharaoh's fourth compromise — let the people go, even the children, but keep back the flocks and herds (the cattle to be held in Egypt, as a pledge of return, or as plunder, the half-release, granting the people, but keeping their substance, the witnesses noting the lesson — another of the world's, and Satan's, compromises: serve God, but leave your possessions, your property, your wealth, in the world, the snare of the partial surrender, keeping back something for the world, not all given to God, the partial obedience God's people must refuse, all to be given to God — the flocks for the sacrifices, and as their own, nothing left in Egypt, Exodus 10:26); Moses would refuse this too (v.26, 'not an hoof left behind') — the full surrender required (all the people and all their substance given to God, the lesson against keeping back any part for the world) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

debates.question: What is the snare in Pharaoh's 'let your flocks and herds be stayed'?

sides.position: The fourth compromise (the people freed, but their substance held) - another of the world's compromises: serve God, but leave your possessions in the world; the snare of keeping back something for the world, the partial surrender God's people must refuse, all given to God (Exodus 10:26, not a hoof left behind)

themes: And Pharaoh called unto Moses, and said, Go ye, serve the LORD; only let your flocks and your herds be stayed: let your little ones also go with you
Pharaoh's fourth compromise - leave the cattle (the crux)
the people freed, but their substance held as a pledge or plunder
the world's snare - serve God but leave your possessions in the world; all to be given to God (Exodus 10:26)

schools.summary: Moses requiring the cattle for sacrifice: 'and Moses said, Thou must give us also sacrifices and burnt offerings, that we may sacrifice unto the LORD our God' — Moses refusing Pharaoh's compromise (v.24): the flocks and herds must go, for they were needed for the sacrifices and burnt offerings to the LORD (the demand for the cattle, essential to the worship, the refusal of the half-release, all the substance to be given to God); the need of the cattle for sacrifice (the burnt offerings to the LORD) — the flocks essential to the worship (the demand for all the substance); 'that we may sacrifice unto the LORD our God' (the worship requiring the offerings, the cattle for the sacrifices, the full surrender, nothing left in Egypt, the refusal of the compromise, all given to God) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: And Moses said, Thou must give us also sacrifices and burnt offerings, that we may sacrifice unto the LORD our God
Moses requiring the cattle for sacrifice
the flocks essential to the worship, the offerings needed (v.24)
the refusal of the half-release, all given to God

schools.summary: Moses' total refusal - not a hoof left behind: 'our cattle also shall go with us; there shall not an hoof be left behind; for thereof must we take to serve the LORD our God; and we know not with what we must serve the LORD, until we come thither' — Moses demanding that all the cattle go, not a single hoof left in Egypt (the total refusal of the compromise, the complete surrender, nothing held back for the world, the famous phrase, 'not an hoof left behind' — the whole substance given to God, the full obedience); the reason (the cattle needed for the service of God) — the worship requiring the offerings (the full surrender, all to be available for God); 'we know not with what we must serve the LORD, until we come thither' (Moses' wisdom, not knowing yet what offerings God would require, all the cattle must go, that they might have whatever God should appoint, the readiness for full obedience, the whole devoted to God, the offerings to be determined by God's command at the place of worship, the lesson — God's people give all to him, holding nothing back, ready to obey whatever he commands, the full consecration) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

debates.question: What does Moses' 'not an hoof left behind' display?

sides.position: The complete surrender to God, nothing held back for the world - all the substance given to God (the full consecration); 'we know not with what we must serve the LORD' - the readiness for full obedience, all devoted to God, ready to obey whatever he appoints

themes: Our cattle also shall go with us; there shall not an hoof be left behind; for thereof must we take to serve the LORD our God; and we know not with what we must serve the LORD, until we come thither
Moses' total refusal - not a hoof left behind
the complete surrender, nothing held back for the world, all given to God
the readiness for full obedience, ready to obey whatever God appoints

schools.summary: Pharaoh hardened again after the darkness: 'but the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart, and he would not let them go' — Pharaoh, after the darkness and the rejected compromise (v.24-26), hardened again (the LORD hardening, the judicial giving-up, as v.20; Exodus 9:12) and refusing to let Israel go (the renewed obstinacy, the broken concession, the hardening confirmed); the LORD hardening (the judicial hardening of the obstinate, after the abuse of the mercies, Romans 9:18) — the divine giving-up (the obstinacy confirmed); 'he would not let them go' (the renewed refusal, the broken concession, the providence, the obstinacy bringing the last and greatest plague, the death of the firstborn, ch. 11-12) — Calvin, Gill, Clarke.

themes: But the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart, and he would not let them go
Pharaoh hardened again after the darkness
the judicial hardening of the obstinate (v.20; Romans 9:18)
the broken concession, the obstinacy bringing the last plague (ch. 11-12)

schools.summary: Pharaoh's threat to Moses: 'and Pharaoh said unto him, Get thee from me, take heed to thyself, see my face no more; for in that day thou seest my face thou shalt die' — Pharaoh, enraged, driving Moses from his presence, forbidding him to come again ('Get thee from me, take heed to thyself, see my face no more,' the wrath of the obstinate king, the rupture final, the threat, no more audiences, the proud king's fury); the threat of death ('in that day thou seest my face thou shalt die') — Pharaoh threatening to kill Moses if he came again (the obstinate fury, the king's defiance to the last); Pharaoh's threat (the final rupture) — the providence (the last interview, the next message, ch. 11, to be the announcement of the death of the firstborn, the king who threatened Moses with death now to lose his own firstborn, the obstinacy bringing the final judgment) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Pharaoh said unto him, Get thee from me, take heed to thyself, see my face no more; for in that day thou seest my face thou shalt die
Pharaoh's threat to Moses
the wrath of the obstinate king, the final rupture
the king who threatened Moses with death now to lose his own firstborn (ch. 11)

schools.summary: Moses' answer that he would see Pharaoh's face no more: 'and Moses said, Thou hast spoken well, I will see thy face again no more' — Moses agreeing with Pharaoh's word: he would indeed see the king's face no more (in this manner, as a suppliant, the negotiations ended, the solemn finality, the last interview before the death of the firstborn, the prophet's dignified answer); Moses' agreement (taking Pharaoh at his word) — the end of the pleadings (the solemn close, the king's obstinacy having sealed his doom); 'I will see thy face again no more' (the last interview, Moses would not come again to plead, the next would be the night of the death of the firstborn, when Pharaoh would summon him, Exodus 12:31, yet, the witnesses harmonize, Moses delivered the final word, ch. 11:4-8, before fully departing, or saw him no more as a petitioner, the solemn finality, the doom sealed, the obstinate king given over to the last plague) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley, Clarke.

themes: And Moses said, Thou hast spoken well, I will see thy face again no more
Moses' answer that he would see Pharaoh's face no more
the last interview, the pleadings ended, the negotiations closed
the king's doom sealed, before the death of the firstborn (Exodus 12:31; ch. 11)
